serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Afghani Mutton Semi Gravy is approximately 200 g, comprising a generous portion of slow-cooked mutton pieces in a creamy, mildly spiced gravy, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Afghani Mutton (Semi Gravy) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Afghani Mutton (Semi Gravy), CR ID G00010846. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.8 g/100g based on known fat composition of mutton and cream; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat data not reported in HPB source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat-based curry with cream and yogurt
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Afghani Mutton Semi Gravy. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat-based curry dishes with cream and yogurt components, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate dishes. Carbohydrate content is 7.44 g/100g. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 5.2 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 467 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods; saturated fat at ~5.8 g/100g further contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced salt in preparation, avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papads, and choose a lower-sodium protein dish as an alternative where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 7.44 g/100g and sugar at 4.69 g/100g are low, supporting glycaemic stability; however, dietary fibre at 0.3 g/100g is negligible and cream adds caloric density.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200 g) paired with a high-fibre side such as salad or dal to improve glycaemic response, while monitoring total calorie intake due to the cream-based preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~5.8 g/100g from mutton and cream, combined with cholesterol at 85 mg/100g and sodium at 467 mg/100g, presents a significant cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced cream in preparation to lower saturated fat, avoid high-sodium accompaniments, and prefer leaner protein alternatives such as grilled chicken or fish for regular meals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), a primary RA disqualifier; saturated fat at ~5.8 g/100g exceeds the 5 g RA threshold and sodium at 467 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or substitute mutton with chicken or fish, request reduced cream to lower saturated fat, and reduce salt in preparation to manage RA-related tissue swelling and inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Afghani Mutton Semi Gravy is a rich, creamy mutton dish inspired by Afghan culinary traditions and widely served in Indian restaurants and food courts across Singapore. The dish features bone-in or boneless mutton pieces slow-cooked in a thick, mildly spiced gravy made primarily from fresh cream and curd (yogurt), seasoned with only three spices — black pepper, garam masala, and chaat masala — giving it a distinctively mild, aromatic, and slightly tangy flavour profile compared to heavier Indian curries. The semi-gravy consistency is thicker than a standard curry but not dry, making it ideal for pairing with naan, roti, or steamed rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat (of which approximately 5.8 g is saturated), 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, while the low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, the elevated sodium (467 mg/100g) and saturated fat (~5.8 g/100g) make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is gluten-free by default. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is popular in Indian and North Indian cuisine segments across Singapore's hawker centres and restaurants.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID G00010846) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known fat composition of mutton and cream components; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong primary source for all other macros and micronutrients. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Afghani Mutton Semi Gravy preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010846) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat at 5.8 g/100g is estimated from the known fat composition of mutton (~40–45% saturated) and cream (~60% saturated fat of fat content), consistent with the HPB-reported total fat of 11.58 g/100g. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable low-carbohydrate, protein-dominant meat-based curry dishes with cream and yogurt. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
